title: Coordination Engineering of Dual-Atom Catalysts for Overall Water Splitting: Mechanistic Insights from Constant-Potential First-Principles and Machine Learning
authors: Jiahang Li, Suhang Li, Chong Yan, Jiajun Yu, Qinzhuang Liu, Ruo-Ya Wang, Dongwei Ma
abstract: The rational design of bifunctional electrocatalysts for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is essential for achieving efficient and cost-effective overall water splitting. Atomically dispersed transition-metal catalysts, including single-atom catalysts and dual-atom catalysts (DACs), have emerged as a prominent class of heterogeneous catalysts, in which coordination engineering plays a decisive role in tuning catalytic performance. Herein, we explore coordination-engineered bifunctional overall water splitting electrocatalysts using graphene-supported DACs (TM1TM2-C6-xNx) as model systems. By tuning C/N coordination and dual-metal combinations (Fe, Co, Ni, and Cu), a library of 228 structures was constructed. A three-step screening strategy, combining constant-charge and constant-potential density functional theory with kinetic analysis of proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET), identifies 24 highly active candidates (TM1TM2 = CoNi, CoCu and Co2) with mixed C/N coordination for OER. These catalysts exhibit overpotentials comparable to that of IrO2 and low PCET barriers (lower than 0.40 eV), among which 22 also show high HER activity. Machine learning reveals clear coordination-dependent structure-performance relationships. Such bifunctionality arises from coordination engineering that enables the simultaneous optimization of OER intermediate adsorption and the hydrogen binding strength for HER. This work establishes coordination engineering as an effective strategy for designing high-performance bifunctional dual-atom electrocatalysts for overall water splitting.
summary: This paper uses a combination of first-principles calculations and machine learning to engineer the coordination environment of dual-atom catalysts on graphene. By tuning the carbon and nitrogen coordination, the researchers successfully optimized both the oxygen and hydrogen evolution reactions, providing a strategy for designing high-performance, non-noble metal catalysts.
main_problem: The challenge of designing efficient, low-cost, bifunctional electrocatalysts for overall water splitting in acidic media by replacing noble metals with transition-metal dual-atom catalysts (DACs).
main_result: The study identified 22 highly active bifunctional DACs (e.g., CoNi, CoCu, Co2) with mixed C/N coordination that exhibit OER overpotentials comparable to IrO2 and low PCET activation barriers.
method: A three-step screening strategy combining constant-charge and constant-potential DFT with kinetic analysis of PCET, integrated with machine learning (Bayesian optimization and SISSO) to identify structure-performance descriptors.
model_or_system: Graphene-supported dual-atom catalysts (TM1TM2-C6-xNx) where TM represents 3d transition metals (Fe, Co, Ni, Cu) and the coordination environment is tuned by varying C/N ratios.
key_observables: OER overpotential (eta_OER), PCET activation barriers (Ea), hydrogen adsorption free energy (deltaG*H), and d-band center (epsilon_d).
important_parameters: Acidic conditions (pH = 1), transition metal species (Fe, Co, Ni, Cu), and C/N coordination ratios.
main_assumptions: The study focuses specifically on the regime of acidic overall water splitting and uses implicit solvation models to represent the electrochemical environment.
figures_summary: The figures likely present the library of 228 structures, the screening results for OER activity, the identification of 24 active candidates, and machine learning-derived descriptors and structure-performance relationships.
paper_structure: Introduction to water splitting challenges; construction of a DAC library; DFT-based screening for OER; kinetic analysis of PCET; machine learning for descriptor identification; evaluation of HER activity; and discussion of the electronic mechanism of bifunctionality.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
